content.title: PayTile — Designing Privacy-First Proximity Payments
content.summary: PayTile is a proximity payments platform — designed and shipped natively across iOS, Android, and Web. I led product design and the full design system. The core problem was one nobody in the payments space had solved — letting two people nearby exchange money without either person handing over a phone number, contact, or any personal information. The result: improvement in privacy comfort and 94% task completion.
content.role: Senior Product Designer · iOS, Android & Web

content.body: PayTile is a proximity payments platform — designed and shipped natively across iOS, Android, and Web. I led product design and the full design system. The core problem was one nobody in the payments space had solved — letting two people nearby exchange money without either person handing over a phone number, contact, or any personal information. The result: improvement in privacy comfort and 94% task completion.

Every existing payment app — Venmo, Cash App, Zelle — requires a phone number or contact sync to send money to someone nearby. Our research found 71% of users wouldn't share their phone number with a stranger they'd just met. For proximity payments to work in the real world the entire privacy model had to be rethought from the ground up.

content.alt: Research visualization: privacy stats, user interviews, competitive audit
content.caption: The privacy gap every existing payment app left open

content.body: We ran 12 user interviews focused on privacy and proximity preferences, six contextual map tests, and a privacy survey of 108 participants. The signal was consistent across every session — anonymity wasn't a nice-to-have. It was the baseline expectation. The fact that no existing app offered it was the gap PayTile was built for.

content.text: The Design Solution — Three-Tier Privacy System

content.body: The solution was a layered privacy model built into every step of the P2P flow. No phone numbers. No contact sync. No QR codes. Just proximity and mutual confirmation — with three tiers of control letting each user decide exactly how much they revealed.

content.caption: Three-tier privacy model — users control exactly how much they reveal at each stage

content.alt: Four iPhone screens showing the complete proximity payment journey
content.caption: Detection to confirmation — no personal information exchanged at any point in the flow

content.text: The Map as the Interaction Surface

content.alt: iPhone screen showing the map in active use with marker hierarchy
content.caption: The map as primary interaction surface — not a background, the product itself

content.body: The map was the product. Every decision about marker hierarchy, proximity indicators, and tile states was made to make the spatial logic feel intuitive without instruction. Users understood what proximity meant just by looking at the screen — no onboarding tooltip required.

content.caption: Design system built for a map-first, privacy-first, cross-platform product

content.body: Purple carried trust and security throughout every screen. Yellow signalled rewards and discovery. Every primary action in the product mapped to one of the two — the colour system was functional, not decorative. Built once in the design system, applied consistently across iOS, Android, and Web without rebuilding for each platform.

content.caption: Privacy-first design validated through research, testing, and real user sessions across iOS and Android

items: Making privacy visible at the exact moment users needed reassurance — not buried in a settings menu — was what moved the comfort metric. Showing the blurred avatar actively protecting them in real time was more convincing than any copy or legal disclaimer could be.

The three-tier consent model came from research not assumption. Early designs had a binary — anonymous or revealed. Testing showed users wanted granular control between the two extremes. The middle tier became the most used option post launch.

Designing the map as a primary interaction surface rather than a utility background changed every decision — marker hierarchy, animation timing, proximity logic — in ways a standard screen-first approach would have missed entirely. The map had to feel alive, not just accurate.
